content:
That would be a nice improvement I think. Right now the function takes err: c_int. First, we are not even sure if it's really an error so I wouldn't call it err. Second, if it is not an error, it should return the value instead of just returning Ok(()), so that we can actually make use of it.

For example, in the code below, I have to first store ret because to_result dumps it.

fn some_function() -> Result<SomeType> {
    let ret = unsafe { bindings::some_ffi_call() };
    to_result(ret).map(|()| SomeType::new(ret))
}

if to_result was returning the value we just passed, the code above would look like this:

fn some_function() -> Result<SomeType> {
    to_result(unsafe { bindings::some_ffi_call() })
        .map(|ret| SomeType::new(ret))
}

Any thoughts?
so you don't like having to_result at all?
I don't think having to go through map is helpful for readability.
It's fine for the methods that return Result<()>

I see, but it's not only about map. As far as I see it doesn't have any valid point to not return the value back. You can also do something like:

let to_handle_later = to_result(unsafe { some_ffi_call() })?;

I think this should be easier to maintain compare to ones with if ret < 0 { return Err } everywhere in the codebase

There are lots of methods out there using to_result as the return value in a method returning Result<()>. I can see having another method that returns an Result<u32, Error> instead, but it should probably be a new method.

I think it makes sense to add a to_result_value with the semantics you describe. For many IO operations that return the number of bytes as a positive value or an error condition as a negative, it makes sense. But other times you are going to map some set of positive values to some kind of enum anyway.
But it might still make sense actually, you can have a match inside your map :shrug:

Second, if it is not an error, it should return the value instead of just returning Ok(()), so that we can actually make use of it.

We discussed having that a long time ago. I had this patch back then -- not intended to be committed, but I rebased it and am copy-pasting it here for reference before I forget about it:

From ae15ff50571e21e0e1a144421fc19f6159d8a79e Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>
Date: Mon, 2 Oct 2023 11:52:50 +0200
Subject: [OLD PATCH] rust: error: return value in `to_result`'s successful path

`to_result` is used to conveniently convert a Linux kernel error
code (an `int`) into a Rust `Result` with no value returned in
the successful case.

In some cases, C APIs return error-or-value integers, i.e. the value
is meaningful and needed afterwards. At the moment, if one wants to
take advantage of `to_result` in this case, one may write code like:

    // SAFETY: ...
    let ret = unsafe { bindings::...() };
    to_result(...ret...)?;
    // Use `ret`.

Returning the error back to the caller in the happy path, i.e. within
the `Ok` variant, allows to shorten code a bit in those cases:

    // SAFETY: ...
    let value = to_result(unsafe { bindings::... })?;

It also encourages to give meaningful names for the local (instead
of generic names like `ret`), since the variable at that point is
not anymore a error-or-value, but the actual integer we need.

In addition, the fact that the returned value is non-negative can
be encoded in the type system, returning an unsigned integer.

Moreover, for all kernel architectures, `sizeof(int) == 4`, so
assuming that the kernel will always be LP64 and that there will be
no ILP128 kernel code, `u32` can be returned. Just in case, add
a static assertion for the future.

Thus make `to_result` return an `u32` with the value.

Suggested-by: Wedson Almeida Filho <wedsonaf@gmail.com> # `c_uint`
Suggested-by: FUJITA Tomonori <fujita.tomonori@gmail.com> # `c_int`
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CANiq72m-9pALVX3gS4N99+xhDK8rS=Wss0H+aBoUyrZTBwmV3w@mail.gmail.com/
Signed-off-by: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>
---
 rust/kernel/error.rs | 28 ++++++++++++++++++++++++----
 1 file changed, 24 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/kernel/error.rs b/rust/kernel/error.rs
index ad31cc92d246..356a84d3a95b 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/error.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/error.rs
@@ -390,12 +390,12 @@ fn from(e: core::convert::Infallible) -> Error {
 /// [Rust documentation]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch09-02-recoverable-errors-with-result.html
 pub type Result<T = (), E = Error> = core::result::Result<T, E>;

-/// Converts an integer as returned by a C kernel function to a [`Result`].
+/// Converts an integer as returned by a C kernel function to a [`Result<u32>`].
 ///
 /// If the integer is negative (i.e. including `< -MAX_ERRNO`), it returns [`Err`] with an [`Error`]
 /// as given by [`Error::from_errno`].
 ///
-/// Otherwise, it returns [`Ok`].
+/// Otherwise, it returns [`Ok`] with the original integer as a [`u32`].
 ///
 /// # Examples
 ///
@@ -424,12 +424,32 @@ fn from(e: core::convert::Infallible) -> Error {
 /// # assert_eq!(f(), Err(EINVAL));
 /// ```
 ///
+/// In cases where a C API returns an interesting value when successful (i.e. not just `0`), the value
+/// can be used (and ideally be named appropriately) without dealing with the original:
+///
+/// ```
+/// # use kernel::error::to_result;
+/// # mod bindings {
+/// #     #![expect(clippy::missing_safety_doc)]
+/// #     use kernel::prelude::*;
+/// #     pub(super) unsafe fn f() -> c_int { 42 }
+/// # }
+/// // SAFETY: ...
+/// let value = to_result(unsafe { bindings::f() })?;
+/// # assert_eq!(value, 42);
+/// # Ok::<(), Error>(())
+/// ```
+///
 /// [`?`]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/operator-expr.html#the-question-mark-operator
-pub fn to_result(err: crate::ffi::c_int) -> Result {
+pub fn to_result(err: crate::ffi::c_int) -> Result<u32> {
     if err < 0 {
         Err(Error::from_errno(err))
     } else {
-        Ok(())
+        // `sizeof(int)` is always 4 in the kernel, thus casting to `u32` is a no-op.
+        kernel::static_assert!(
+            core::mem::size_of::<crate::ffi::c_int>() == core::mem::size_of::<u32>()
+        );
+        Ok(err as u32)
     }
 }

--
2.51.0

It is based on other 2 patches I had in a longer series -- those 2 I can submit, since they are improvements that do not depend on this, so I will do that.

Back then we also discussed the topic of returning it as unsigned already or not, since we know it cannot be negative.

Back then we also discussed having to_results taking and returning different types, e.g. for passing C longs into it and returning usize (which would now be easier, since the FFI types are now proper).

For the "use the result to return in a -> Result function", it is true nowadays we have more of those (~39). In the examples I was adding in one of the other patches, what I show was to do this:

/// fn f() -> Result {
///     // SAFETY: ...
///     to_result(unsafe { bindings::f1() })?;
///
///     // SAFETY: ...
///     to_result(unsafe { bindings::f2() })?;
///
///     // ...
///
///     Ok(())
/// }

rather than simplifying by removing the ?; Ok(()), but I think as a separate function is fine too.

Cc @FUJITA Tomonori who suggested it among others, as mentioned in the commit message.

The other two patches I referred to, for context (I will finish cleaning them up and send them):

From 106c5d95a3fb36941b9082ca2908bb1bcc397458 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>
Date: Mon, 2 Oct 2023 13:41:18 +0200
Subject: [PATCH 1/2] rust: error: improve `Error::from_errno` documentation

This constructor is nowadays public, thus improve its documentation,
including adding examples, since we will refer to it from `to_result`
in a later commit.

Signed-off-by: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>
---
 rust/kernel/error.rs | 19 +++++++++++++++++--
 1 file changed, 17 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/kernel/error.rs b/rust/kernel/error.rs
index a41de293dcd1..c415c3d3a3b6 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/error.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/error.rs
@@ -101,8 +101,23 @@ macro_rules! declare_err {
 impl Error {
     /// Creates an [`Error`] from a kernel error code.
     ///
-    /// It is a bug to pass an out-of-range `errno`. `EINVAL` would
-    /// be returned in such a case.
+    /// `errno` must be within error code range (i.e. `>= -MAX_ERRNO && < 0`).
+    ///
+    /// It is a bug to pass an out-of-range `errno`. [`code::EINVAL`] is returned in such a case.
+    ///
+    /// # Examples
+    ///
+    /// ```
+    /// assert_eq!(Error::from_errno(-1), EPERM);
+    /// assert_eq!(Error::from_errno(-2), ENOENT);
+    /// ```
+    ///
+    /// The following calls are considered a bug:
+    ///
+    /// ```
+    /// assert_eq!(Error::from_errno(0), EINVAL);
+    /// assert_eq!(Error::from_errno(-1000000), EINVAL);
+    /// ```
     pub fn from_errno(errno: crate::ffi::c_int) -> Error {
         if let Some(error) = Self::try_from_errno(errno) {
             error
--
2.51.0

From 18f7fff3cee3cc18031685edb3b4fc5eb89c7532 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>
Date: Sun, 1 Oct 2023 23:48:23 +0200
Subject: [PATCH 2/2] rust: error: improve `to_result` documentation

The function will start returning a value in the successful path
in the next commit, thus improve the documentation for the existing
functionality here, including adding an example of how to perform
early returns with it.

Signed-off-by: Miguel Ojeda <ojeda@kernel.org>
---
 rust/kernel/error.rs | 37 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++--
 1 file changed, 35 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/rust/kernel/error.rs b/rust/kernel/error.rs
index c415c3d3a3b6..ad31cc92d246 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/error.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/error.rs
@@ -390,8 +390,41 @@ fn from(e: core::convert::Infallible) -> Error {
 /// [Rust documentation]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch09-02-recoverable-errors-with-result.html
 pub type Result<T = (), E = Error> = core::result::Result<T, E>;

-/// Converts an integer as returned by a C kernel function to an error if it's negative, and
-/// `Ok(())` otherwise.
+/// Converts an integer as returned by a C kernel function to a [`Result`].
+///
+/// If the integer is negative (i.e. including `< -MAX_ERRNO`), it returns [`Err`] with an [`Error`]
+/// as given by [`Error::from_errno`].
+///
+/// Otherwise, it returns [`Ok`].
+///
+/// # Examples
+///
+/// This function may be used to easily perform early returns with the [`?`] operator when working
+/// with C APIs within Rust abstractions:
+///
+/// ```
+/// # use kernel::error::to_result;
+/// # mod bindings {
+/// #     #![expect(clippy::missing_safety_doc)]
+/// #     use kernel::prelude::*;
+/// #     pub(super) unsafe fn f1() -> c_int { 0 }
+/// #     pub(super) unsafe fn f2() -> c_int { EINVAL.to_errno() }
+/// # }
+/// fn f() -> Result {
+///     // SAFETY: ...
+///     to_result(unsafe { bindings::f1() })?;
+///
+///     // SAFETY: ...
+///     to_result(unsafe { bindings::f2() })?;
+///
+///     // ...
+///
+///     Ok(())
+/// }
+/// # assert_eq!(f(), Err(EINVAL));
+/// ```
+///
+/// [`?`]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/operator-expr.html#the-question-mark-operator
 pub fn to_result(err: crate::ffi::c_int) -> Result {
     if err < 0 {
         Err(Error::from_errno(err))
--
2.51.0
When I worked on PHY abstractions, this topic was discussed, I guess. I did grep to find out if such is still necessary. PHY abstractions doesn't use Result<u32> but Result<u16>.

FUJITA Tomonori said:

When I worked on PHY abstractions, this topic was discussed, I guess. I did grep to find out if such is still necessary. PHY abstractions doesn't use Result<u32> but Result<u16>.

That's another limitation of to_result. It only supports i32, so we have to cast other types to i32 in order to use to_result, and even that isn't always possible in every case (e.g., see https://lore.kernel.org/all/20250822080252.773d6f54@nimda.home/).

So in addition to returning the given number, we could make it generic to pass any type of number on to_result and it will the same type back, so things like Result<u16> (and https://lore.kernel.org/all/20250822080252.773d6f54@nimda.home) can work.

A quick/rough to_result P.o.C would look something like this:

pub type Result<T, E = ()> = core::result::Result<T, E>;

pub fn to_result<T>(v: T) -> Result<T>
where
    T: PartialOrd + Default + Copy,
{
    if v < T::default() { Err(()) } else { Ok(v) }
}

fn main() {
    let x = to_result(1i32).unwrap();
    let y = to_result(5i16).unwrap();
    let z = to_result(10i8).unwrap();
    let t = to_result(42u32).unwrap();
}

(It could probably be implemented in a much better way, I don't have time to think about it in more detail today.)

Yeah, previously we discussed using a custom trait implemented on primitive integers, and an associated output type.

Nowadays with the new FFI types that is a easier. However, it could perhaps be potentially nice to return the unsigned one, or at least back then we discussed that (please see above). I also found old minutes at https://hackmd.io/wBb1XQAIQ_efn0iaYJBssA, but without much detail, sadly.

Thanks!

Here is my proposal on to_result:

diff --git a/rust/kernel/error.rs b/rust/kernel/error.rs
index a41de293dcd1..6563ea71e203 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/error.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/error.rs
@@ -376,12 +376,19 @@ fn from(e: core::convert::Infallible) -> Error {
 pub type Result<T = (), E = Error> = core::result::Result<T, E>;

 /// Converts an integer as returned by a C kernel function to an error if it's negative, and
-/// `Ok(())` otherwise.
-pub fn to_result(err: crate::ffi::c_int) -> Result {
-    if err < 0 {
-        Err(Error::from_errno(err))
+/// returns the original value otherwise.
+pub fn to_result<T>(code: T) -> Result<T>
+where
+    T: Copy + TryInto<i32>,
+{
+    // Try casting into `i32`.
+    let casted: crate::ffi::c_int = code.try_into().unwrap_or(0);
+
+    if casted < 0 {
+        Err(Error::from_errno(casted))
     } else {
-        Ok(())
+        // Return the original input value.
+        Ok(code)
     }
 }

Functions that return () would be updated as:

to_result($something).map(|_| ())

or

to_result($something)?;
Ok(())
In my opinion the return type of the caller functions shouldn't influence the type of this function, it shouldn't be necessary to create extra function next to current to_result
It also removes the need of casting other numeric types into i32 from the caller side

Onur Özkan said:

It also removes the need of casting other numeric types into i32 from the caller side

Which can help cases like this: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20250822080252.773d6f54@nimda.home
